    • Maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM): a list of all dependencies, their versions, and their transitive dependencies. When a new vulnerability is announced, you can instantly determine if you're affected.
    • Patching cadence: critical vulnerabilities within 24-48 hours. High within a week. Medium within a month. Establish this as organizational policy.

7. Identification and Authentication Failures (A07:2021):

Weak authentication mechanisms. Credential stuffing, brute force, session hijacking, missing MFA.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Credential stuffing: attackers use breached username/password pairs from other sites. If your users reuse passwords, their accounts are compromised. Mitigation: MFA, breached password detection (check passwords against known breach databases — HaveIBeenPwned API), rate limiting on login attempts.
    • Brute force: automated attempts to guess passwords. Mitigation: account lockout after N failed attempts (but be careful — this can be used to lock out legitimate users as a DoS vector), CAPTCHA after failed attempts, exponential backoff on login attempts.
    • Session fixation: attacker sets a session ID before the user authenticates. Mitigation: regenerate session ID upon authentication.

8. Software and Data Integrity Failures (A08:2021):

Code and infrastructure that doesn't verify integrity. Using untrusted CDN scripts without integrity checks, insecure CI/CD pipelines, auto-updates without verification.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Subresource Integrity (SRI): <script src="cdn-url" integrity="sha384-hash"> — the browser verifies the script hash before executing. Prevents CDN compromise from injecting malicious code.
    • CI/CD pipeline security: the pipeline has access to production credentials, deployment tools, and artifact registries. A compromised pipeline can deploy malicious code to production. Protect: restrict pipeline access, require code review before merge, sign artifacts, verify signatures before deployment.
    • Supply chain attacks: SolarWinds (compromised build system injected malware into updates), dependency confusion (publishing malicious packages with internal package names to public registries), typosquatting (registering package names similar to popular packages).

9. Security Logging and Monitoring Failures (A09:2021):

Insufficient logging of security events. No monitoring for suspicious activity. Breaches go undetected for weeks or months.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Average time to detect a breach: ~200 days (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report). Effective logging and monitoring dramatically reduces this.
    • What to log (security-relevant events): all authentication events (success AND failure), all authorization failures, all access to sensitive data, all administrative actions, all input validation failures, all exceptions in security-critical code paths.
    • What NOT to log: passwords, tokens, session IDs (in plaintext), credit card numbers, PII without masking.
    • SIEM (Security Information and Event Management): centralized platform that collects security logs from all systems, correlates events, detects patterns, and alerts on suspicious activity. Splunk, Elastic SIEM, Azure Sentinel, AWS Security Hub, Sumo Logic.
    • Security alerting: failed login burst from same IP (credential stuffing), login from unusual geolocation, privilege escalation attempts, access to sensitive resources outside business hours, large data export/download.

10. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) (A10:2021):

The application makes HTTP requests to a URL controlled by the attacker. The attacker tricks the server into accessing internal resources.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Example: an application has a "fetch URL preview" feature. The attacker submits http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (the cloud instance metadata endpoint). The server fetches it and returns IAM credentials, private IPs, and other sensitive data.
    • The AWS instance metadata service is the most exploited SSRF target. IMDSv2 (requires a token obtained via PUT request — not achievable through simple SSRF) mitigates this.
    • Prevention: validate and sanitize all URLs. Whitelist allowed domains. Block access to internal IP ranges (10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x, 192.168.x.x, 169.254.x.x). Use IMDSv2 on all cloud instances. Don't expose raw URL fetch functionality to users.

6. Data Security and Encryption

Encryption at Rest

What it is: Data stored on disk, in databases, in object storage, on backup media is encrypted. An attacker who gains physical access to the storage (stolen disk, compromised storage account) cannot read the data without the encryption key.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Transparent encryption (server-side): The storage system encrypts/decrypts automatically. The application doesn't need to change. AWS S3 Server-Side Encryption, RDS encryption, Azure Storage Service Encryption. This is the minimum — enable it on everything.
    • Application-level encryption: The application encrypts specific sensitive fields before storing them. The database sees ciphertext, not plaintext. Even a database administrator cannot read the encrypted fields. Use for: PII, financial data, health records, or any data where database-level access shouldn't reveal the plaintext.
    • Client-side encryption: Data is encrypted before it's sent to the cloud. The cloud provider never sees plaintext. Maximum security but prevents server-side processing (can't search, index, or query encrypted data without decrypting).
    • Encryption algorithms: AES-256-GCM (symmetric, authenticated — the standard for data at rest). Never use DES, 3DES, RC4, or AES-ECB (Electronic Codebook mode leaks patterns).

Encryption in Transit

What it is: Data moving across the network is encrypted. TLS for HTTP (HTTPS), TLS for database connections, TLS for message broker connections, mTLS for service-to-service.

  • Interview-specific:
    • TLS 1.2 is the minimum acceptable version. TLS 1.3 is preferred (faster handshake, forward secrecy by default, removed vulnerable cipher suites).
    • Certificate management: automate issuance and renewal (AWS ACM, Let's Encrypt, cert-manager in Kubernetes). Monitor for certificate expiry. An expired certificate = an outage.
    • Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS): even if the server's private key is compromised in the future, past recorded traffic cannot be decrypted. TLS 1.3 mandates PFS. TLS 1.2 supports it with ECDHE cipher suites.
    • Internal traffic: many organizations encrypt external traffic (client to load balancer) but leave internal traffic (load balancer to application, service to service) unencrypted. In zero-trust architecture, ALL traffic is encrypted — internal and external. Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) handles this transparently with mTLS.

Key Management

  • Interview-specific:
    • Never hardcode keys. Not in code, not in configuration files, not in environment variables baked into images, not in Git. Ever. The most common cause of key compromise is keys committed to source control.
    • Key Management Service (KMS): AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, GCP Cloud KMS, HashiCorp Vault. Keys are stored in hardware security modules (HSMs). Applications request encryption/decryption operations — the key never leaves the KMS.
    • Envelope encryption: Encrypt data with a Data Encryption Key (DEK). Encrypt the DEK with a Key Encryption Key (KEK) stored in KMS. Store the encrypted DEK alongside the encrypted data. To decrypt: send the encrypted DEK to KMS → KMS decrypts with KEK → use the DEK to decrypt the data. This avoids sending large amounts of data to KMS (only the small DEK is sent).
    • Key rotation: Rotate keys periodically (annually at minimum, more frequently for sensitive data). With envelope encryption, rotating the KEK only requires re-encrypting the DEKs, not all the data. Plan for both routine rotation and emergency rotation (if a key is potentially compromised).
    • Key hierarchy: Root key (stored in HSM, never exported) → Master keys (per service or per environment) → Data encryption keys (per record or per file). Compromise of a data encryption key affects only the data encrypted with that key, not all data in the system.

Data Masking and Tokenization

Data Masking: Replace sensitive data with fictitious but realistic data. A credit card number becomes ****-****-****-1234. An email becomes a***@example.com. A name becomes a random name.

Types: static masking (create a masked copy of a database for non-production use), dynamic masking (mask data in real-time based on the requester's permissions — an admin sees the full SSN, a support agent sees ***-**-1234).

Tokenization: Replace sensitive data with a non-sensitive token. The mapping between token and real data is stored in a secure token vault. The token has no mathematical relationship to the original data (unlike encryption, which can be reversed with a key).

  • Interview-specific:
    • PCI-DSS compliance: most organizations tokenize credit card numbers. The card number is sent to a tokenization service (often the payment processor — Stripe, Adyen). A token is returned. The application stores only the token. The real card number is never in the application's database.
    • Dynamic masking is how you implement field-level authorization for sensitive data. A customer support agent querying a user profile sees masked SSN and masked credit card number. A fraud analyst querying the same profile sees unmasked data. Same query, different visibility based on role.

Data Classification

  • Interview-specific:
    • Every organization should classify data by sensitivity: Public (marketing content, public docs), Internal (employee directory, internal wikis), Confidential (customer data, financial data, business strategy), Restricted (credit card numbers, health records, authentication credentials, encryption keys).
    • Classification drives security controls: Restricted data requires encryption at rest AND in transit, access logging, MFA for access, strict retention policies, and annual access reviews. Public data requires none of these.
    • Automated classification: scan databases and file stores for patterns matching PII (email patterns, phone numbers, SSN patterns, credit card numbers). Tools: AWS Macie, Azure Purview, Google DLP API.

7. API Security

API Threat Landscape

  • Interview-specific: APIs are the #1 attack surface for modern applications. OWASP has a dedicated API Security Top 10:

Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA/IDOR): The most common API vulnerability. GET /api/users/123/bank-accounts — changing 123 to 456 returns another user's bank accounts. The API doesn't verify that the authenticated user owns the requested resource.

Fix: on every request, verify that the authenticated user has access to the specific resource being requested. Not just "is this user authenticated?" but "is this user authorized to access THIS specific resource?"

Broken Authentication: Weak token generation, missing token validation, credentials transmitted over HTTP, no rate limiting on authentication endpoints.

Broken Object Property Level Authorization: API returns more data than the client needs. Internal fields (isAdmin, internalNotes, costPrice) are included in the response. An attacker reads or modifies properties they shouldn't have access to.

Fix: explicitly define response schemas. Return ONLY the fields the client is authorized to see. Use different response DTOs for different authorization levels.

Unrestricted Resource Consumption: No rate limiting, no pagination limits, no maximum payload size. An attacker can exhaust server resources with expensive queries, massive uploads, or rapid-fire requests.

Fix: rate limiting per client/API key, pagination with maximum page size, request payload size limits, query complexity limits (for GraphQL), timeout on expensive operations.

Broken Function Level Authorization: A regular user can access admin endpoints. POST /api/admin/users/delete is accessible to anyone with a valid token because the endpoint doesn't check the user's role.

Fix: authorization middleware on EVERY endpoint. Deny by default. Define which roles can access which endpoints. Test that unauthorized users receive 403.

API Security Best Practices

  • Interview-specific:
    • Authentication for every endpoint. No anonymous endpoints except public content, health checks, and the login/register flow itself.
    • HTTPS only. Redirect all HTTP to HTTPS. Use HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) headers to prevent downgrade attacks.
    • Rate limiting. Per-client, per-endpoint. Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header. Implement at the API gateway level.
    • Input validation. Validate every input field against expected type, format, length, and range. Use a schema validation library. Reject anything that doesn't conform. Validate on the server — client-side validation is a UX feature, not a security control.
    • Output encoding. Encode output to prevent XSS when API responses are rendered in browsers.
    • API versioning. Version your API. Deprecate old versions with adequate notice. Don't leave old, potentially vulnerable API versions running indefinitely.
    • API keys vs OAuth tokens. API keys identify the calling application (for rate limiting, billing). OAuth tokens identify and authorize the user. API keys alone are insufficient for user-level authorization.
    • Logging and monitoring. Log every API request (method, path, authenticated user, response code, latency). Monitor for: unusual traffic patterns, spike in 401/403 responses, excessive 500 responses, traffic from blocked IPs.
    • CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing). Configure which domains can call your API from a browser. NEVER use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * for APIs that handle authenticated requests.

GraphQL Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • Query complexity limiting: GraphQL allows clients to request deeply nested data. A malicious query can request 10 levels of nesting, causing the server to join and fetch enormous amounts of data. Set a maximum query depth and complexity score. Reject queries exceeding the limit.
    • Introspection in production: GraphQL introspection allows anyone to discover your entire schema. Disable introspection in production (or restrict to authenticated admin users).
    • Batching attacks: GraphQL allows multiple queries in a single request. An attacker can batch thousands of login attempts in one HTTP request, bypassing per-request rate limiting. Rate limit by operation count, not just request count.
    • Authorization per field: Different fields in the same type may have different access levels. A User type might have email (public), phoneNumber (self and admin), and salary (admin only). Implement field-level authorization resolvers.

8. Infrastructure Security

Container Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • Minimal base images: Use distroless or Alpine-based images. Fewer packages = fewer vulnerabilities = smaller attack surface. A full Ubuntu image has hundreds of packages you don't need, each a potential vulnerability.
    • Don't run as root: Configure containers to run as a non-root user. If an attacker exploits the application, they have non-root access inside the container — limiting what they can do. In Kubernetes: securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true.
    • Read-only filesystem: Make the container filesystem read-only (readOnlyRootFilesystem: true). If an attacker gains access, they can't write malicious files. Use ephemeral volumes (emptyDir) for any necessary temporary storage.
    • No privileged containers: privileged: true gives the container full access to the host. NEVER use in production unless absolutely necessary (and audit heavily if so).
    • Image scanning: Scan images for vulnerabilities in the CI/CD pipeline (Trivy, Snyk Container, ECR scanning). Block deployment of images with critical vulnerabilities.
    • Image signing and verification: Sign images after building. Verify signatures before deploying. Prevents running tampered images. Tools: Cosign (from Sigstore), Notary.
    • Private registry: Store images in a private container registry (ECR, GCR, ACR, Harbor). Don't pull from public registries in production without vetting.
    • No secrets in images: Never bake credentials, API keys, or certificates into container images. Inject at runtime from secrets management.

Kubernetes Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • RBAC (Kubernetes RBAC): Define who can do what in the cluster. Roles (namespace-scoped) and ClusterRoles (cluster-wide) grant permissions. RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings assign roles to users or service accounts. Principle: minimum necessary permissions. Don't give developers cluster-admin in production.
    • Network Policies: Default Kubernetes networking allows all pods to communicate with all other pods. Network Policies restrict this: "only pods with label app=frontend can communicate with pods with label app=api on port 8080." Requires a CNI plugin that supports Network Policies (Calico, Cilium).
    • Pod Security Standards (PSS): Define security profiles for pods. Privileged (unrestricted — only for system pods), Baseline (prevents known privilege escalations), Restricted (hardened — no root, read-only filesystem, no host networking). Enforced via Pod Security Admission controller.
    • Service accounts: Every pod runs with a service account. Don't use the default service account (it may have more permissions than needed). Create dedicated service accounts per workload with least-privilege RBAC.
    • Secrets management in K8s: Native Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded, NOT encrypted by default. They're stored in etcd in plaintext (unless etcd encryption is configured). For production: use External Secrets Operator (syncs from Vault/AWS Secrets Manager), Sealed Secrets (encrypted in Git, decrypted in cluster), or mount secrets directly from Vault via sidecar.
    • etcd security: etcd holds all cluster state including secrets. Encrypt etcd at rest. Restrict network access to etcd. Enable authentication.
    • Admission controllers: Intercept API requests before they're persisted. OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno can enforce policies: "all containers must have resource limits," "no container can use the 'latest' tag," "all pods must have a network policy."

Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM)

  • Interview-specific:
    • The most common security issues in cloud environments are misconfigurations, not sophisticated attacks. Public S3 buckets, overly permissive IAM roles, unencrypted databases, open security groups.
    • CSPM tools continuously scan cloud environments for misconfigurations: AWS Security Hub, Prisma Cloud, Prowler (open source for AWS), Checkov (IaC scanning), ScoutSuite.
    • IaC scanning: scan Terraform, CloudFormation, and Kubernetes manifests for security issues BEFORE deployment. Checkov, tfsec, Trivy (IaC mode). Shift left — catch misconfigurations in the pull request, not in production.
    • Cloud trail/audit logging: enable CloudTrail (AWS), Azure Activity Log, GCP Audit Logs. Log every API call to the cloud provider. Who created what resource, when, from where? Essential for incident investigation and compliance.

Supply Chain Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • Software Bill of Materials (SBOM): A complete inventory of all components in your software — direct dependencies, transitive dependencies, their versions. When a vulnerability is announced, an SBOM tells you instantly whether you're affected.
    • Dependency scanning: Scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities in every CI build. Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check. Fail the build on critical findings.
    • Dependency pinning: Pin exact dependency versions in lock files (package-lock.json, Pipfile.lock, go.sum). Prevents supply chain attacks where a compromised dependency publishes a malicious update.
    • Artifact signing: Sign build artifacts (container images, packages) with a private key. Verify the signature before deployment. Ensures the artifact wasn't tampered with between build and deployment. Cosign + Sigstore for container images.
    • Build provenance: Record how an artifact was built — which source code, which build system, which builder identity. SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) is a framework for supply chain security. Level 1 (build process exists) → Level 4 (hermetic, reproducible, non-forgeable build with two-party review).
    • Private package registries: Host internal packages in a private registry. Configure package managers to check the private registry first. Prevents dependency confusion attacks (attacker publishes a malicious package with the same name as your internal package to a public registry).

9. Security in CI/CD Pipelines

Pipeline Security Risks

  • Interview-specific: The CI/CD pipeline is one of the most privileged components in your infrastructure. It has access to: source code, production credentials, deployment tools, artifact registries, and potentially production environments. A compromised pipeline can deploy arbitrary code to production.

DevSecOps Practices

Shift Left: Integrate security testing as early as possible in the development lifecycle. Don't wait for a pre-production security review. Catch vulnerabilities in the developer's IDE, in the pull request, in the CI build.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Pre-commit hooks: Run linters and basic security checks before code is committed. Detect hardcoded secrets (git-secrets, detect-secrets, truffleHog), check for known vulnerable code patterns.
    • SAST (Static Application Security Testing): Analyze source code for vulnerabilities without executing it. Finds: SQL injection, XSS, insecure deserialization, hardcoded credentials, buffer overflows. Tools: SonarQube, Semgrep, CodeQL (GitHub), Checkmarx. Run in CI on every pull request.
    • SCA (Software Composition Analysis): Scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities. Tools: Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check. Run in CI on every build. Fail on critical vulnerabilities.
    • DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing): Test the running application by sending malicious requests. Finds: injection vulnerabilities, authentication flaws, misconfiguration. Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite. Run against staging environment.
    • IaC Scanning: Scan infrastructure code for security misconfigurations. Tools: Checkov, tfsec, Trivy. Run in CI on every Terraform/CloudFormation change. Catch "S3 bucket without encryption" before it reaches production.
    • Container Image Scanning: Scan built images for OS and application vulnerabilities. Tools: Trivy, Snyk Container, Grype. Run after building, before pushing to registry. Block images with critical vulnerabilities.
    • Secret Scanning: Detect secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens) in source code and commit history. Tools: GitGuardian, GitHub Secret Scanning, truffleHog. Run on every commit. Alert immediately on detection.

Pipeline hardening:

  • Interview-specific:
    • Minimal pipeline permissions: The pipeline should have only the permissions it needs. Separate pipelines for building (needs code read, artifact write) and deploying (needs deployment permissions). Don't give the build pipeline production deployment access.
    • Ephemeral build environments: Each build runs in a fresh, disposable environment. No state carries over between builds. Prevents an attacker from persisting in the build environment.
    • Immutable artifacts: Once an artifact (container image, package) is built and signed, it's never modified. The same artifact goes through testing, staging, and production. No rebuilding between environments — what you tested is exactly what you deploy.
    • Audit trail: Log every pipeline execution, every deployment, every configuration change. Who triggered the build, what code was included, what tests ran, what was deployed where. This audit trail is essential for incident investigation and compliance.
    • Separation of duties: Developers write code. The pipeline builds and tests. A different pipeline (or approval gate) deploys to production. No single person can push code directly to production without review.
    • Branch protection: Require code review (minimum 2 approvers for sensitive repositories), require CI passing (all security scans green), require signed commits. No direct pushes to main/production branches.

10. Incident Response and Security Operations

Incident Response Framework

  • Interview-specific: When asked "what do you do when a security breach is detected?" having a structured framework demonstrates maturity.

Phase 1 — Preparation (Before any incident):

  • Incident response plan: documented, tested, rehearsed. Who does what, communication channels, escalation paths.
  • Incident response team: defined roles — incident commander (coordinates), technical lead (investigates and remediates), communications lead (internal and external), legal (regulatory obligations).
  • Playbooks: pre-written procedures for common incident types. "Compromised credentials playbook," "Data breach playbook," "Ransomware playbook," "DDoS playbook." Each playbook has specific steps, not generic guidance.
  • Tools and access: ensure the IR team has access to logs, can isolate systems, can revoke credentials, can communicate out-of-band (if the primary communication system is compromised).
  • Tabletop exercises: simulate incidents without actual system changes. "The payment database was breached at 2 AM. Walk through what you would do." Run quarterly.

Phase 2 — Detection and Analysis:

  • How was the incident detected? SIEM alert, anomaly detection, customer report, third-party notification, threat intelligence.
  • Scope assessment: what systems are affected? What data may be compromised? How many users are impacted? Is the attacker still active?
  • Evidence preservation: capture logs, memory dumps, disk images BEFORE making changes. Evidence may be needed for legal proceedings.
  • Classification: severity (P1-P4), type (data breach, unauthorized access, malware, DDoS).

Phase 3 — Containment:

  • Short-term containment: stop the bleeding. Isolate compromised systems (remove from network), revoke compromised credentials, block attacker IP addresses, disable compromised accounts.
  • Long-term containment: implement temporary fixes that allow business operations to continue while you work on eradication. Stand up clean replacement systems if needed.

Phase 4 — Eradication:

  • Remove the root cause. Patch the vulnerability. Remove malware. Close the entry point. If credentials were stolen, rotate ALL potentially affected credentials (not just confirmed ones).

Phase 5 — Recovery:

  • Restore systems from clean backups if needed. Bring systems back online gradually. Monitor closely for signs of continued compromise.
  • Verify: is the vulnerability actually fixed? Is the attacker actually out? Sometimes attackers establish persistence mechanisms (backdoor accounts, scheduled tasks) that survive initial remediation.

Phase 6 — Post-Incident Review (Blameless):

  • What happened? Timeline of events.
  • How was it detected? Could we have detected it faster?
  • What was the root cause? Was it a code bug, a misconfiguration, a process failure, a social engineering attack?
  • What mitigations prevent recurrence?
  • What did we learn? Update playbooks, improve detection, fix process gaps.
  • The review must be blameless — focus on systemic failures, not individual mistakes. If someone made an error, ask "why did the system allow that error to have this impact?" not "who did this wrong?"

Security Monitoring

  • Interview-specific:
    • SIEM (Security Information and Event Management): Collects logs from all systems (application logs, authentication logs, network logs, cloud API logs, endpoint logs). Correlates events across sources. Detects patterns (same IP failing authentication across multiple services, unusual data access patterns, privilege escalation sequences). Alerts on suspicious activity.
    • SOC (Security Operations Center): A team that monitors security alerts 24/7. Triages alerts, investigates incidents, escalates as needed. Can be in-house or outsourced (MSSP — Managed Security Service Provider).
    • Detection engineering: Writing detection rules based on known attack techniques (MITRE ATT&CK framework). "Detect when a service account makes API calls it has never made before." "Detect when data download volume exceeds 10x the 30-day average." These rules are developed, tested, and maintained like code.
    • Threat intelligence: Information about known threat actors, their techniques, their infrastructure (IP addresses, domains, malware hashes). Feed threat intelligence into your SIEM to detect known indicators of compromise.

Breach Notification Requirements

  • Interview-specific:
    • GDPR: notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach. Notify affected individuals "without undue delay" if the breach is likely to result in high risk to their rights.
    • HIPAA: notify affected individuals within 60 days. Notify HHS (Department of Health and Human Services). If breach affects 500+ individuals, notify media.
    • PCI-DSS: notify the card brands and the acquiring bank immediately upon detection of a breach involving cardholder data.
    • State laws (US): most states have breach notification laws with varying timelines (24 hours to 60 days).
    • Having a pre-prepared breach communication template, legal counsel on retainer, and a clear notification workflow saves critical hours during an actual breach.

11. Compliance and Regulatory Security

Major Regulatory Frameworks

GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation):

  • Interview-specific:
    • Applies to any organization processing personal data of EU residents, regardless of where the organization is located.
    • Key rights: right to access (users can request all data held about them), right to erasure (right to be forgotten — delete all personal data on request), right to data portability (export data in machine-readable format), right to object (opt out of processing for marketing), right to rectification (correct inaccurate data).
    • Principles: data minimization (collect only what you need), purpose limitation (use data only for stated purposes), storage limitation (don't keep data longer than needed), accuracy (keep data accurate), integrity and confidentiality (protect data appropriately).
    • Technical implications: you must know where ALL personal data lives across ALL systems (data inventory, data lineage). You must be able to delete a specific user's data from EVERY system — primary databases, replicas, caches, backups, analytics warehouses, search indexes, log files. This is extremely hard in microservices architectures with dozens of data stores.
    • Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA): required for high-risk processing (large-scale profiling, systematic monitoring, sensitive data processing). Identify risks and mitigations before starting processing.
    • Data Protection Officer (DPO): required for organizations that process personal data at scale.
    • Fines: up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million, whichever is higher.

PCI-DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard):

  • Interview-specific:
    • Applies to any organization that processes, stores, or transmits credit card data.
    • 12 requirements covering: network security, data protection, vulnerability management, access control, monitoring, and security policies.
    • The simplest compliance strategy: DON'T store cardholder data. Use a PCI-compliant payment processor (Stripe, Adyen, Braintree). Tokenize card numbers at the processor. Your system only handles tokens — dramatically reducing PCI scope.
    • If you must store cardholder data: encrypt with strong cryptography, restrict access on a need-to-know basis, log all access, perform regular vulnerability scanning and penetration testing, maintain a security policy.
    • Annual assessment: Self-Assessment Questionnaire (SAQ) for smaller merchants. External audit by a Qualified Security Assessor (QSA) for larger merchants and service providers.

HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act):

  • Interview-specific:
    • Applies to healthcare providers, health plans, and their business associates in the US.
    • Protected Health Information (PHI): any individually identifiable health information. Name + diagnosis = PHI. Anonymized health data is not PHI.
    • Technical safeguards: encryption (at rest and in transit), access controls (role-based, minimum necessary), audit logging (who accessed what PHI when), integrity controls (prevent unauthorized modification), transmission security (encrypt all PHI in transit).
    • Business Associate Agreement (BAA): if you process PHI on behalf of a covered entity, you must sign a BAA. Cloud providers offer BAAs (AWS, Azure, GCP all sign BAAs for HIPAA-eligible services — but not all services are HIPAA-eligible).

SOC 2 (Service Organization Control 2):

  • Interview-specific:
    • Not a regulation — a voluntary audit standard. But increasingly required by enterprise customers before they'll use your service.
    • Based on Trust Service Criteria: Security (mandatory), Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy (optional criteria).
    • Type I: snapshot — are controls designed properly at a point in time?
    • Type II: ongoing — are controls operating effectively over a period (typically 6-12 months)?
    • SOC 2 Type II is the gold standard for SaaS companies. Enterprise customers request it during procurement. It's a significant investment (6-12 months to prepare, $50,000-200,000 in audit costs) but often a business requirement.

Compliance as Code

  • Interview-specific: Manual compliance is unsustainable. Automate compliance checks:
    • Policy as code: Define compliance rules as automated checks. OPA policies, AWS Config rules, Azure Policy, Checkov rules. "All databases must be encrypted." "All S3 buckets must have versioning enabled." "No security groups allow SSH from 0.0.0.0/0."
    • Continuous compliance monitoring: Don't check compliance once a year for the audit. Check continuously — every resource, every day. Alert on non-compliance immediately. Auto-remediate where possible (automatically enable encryption on a newly created unencrypted database).
    • Audit evidence collection: Automate the collection of evidence for auditors. Export CloudTrail logs, Config compliance reports, access review results, vulnerability scan reports. This reduces the burden on engineering during audit periods.

12. Security Testing

Types of Security Testing

SAST (Static Application Security Testing):

  • Analyzes source code without executing it
  • Finds: injection vulnerabilities, hardcoded secrets, insecure patterns, buffer overflows
  • Fast, cheap, can run on every pull request
  • High false positive rate — requires tuning
  • Tools: SonarQube, Semgrep, CodeQL, Checkmarx, Fortify

DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing):

  • Tests the running application by sending malicious requests
  • Finds: injection, authentication flaws, misconfiguration, SSRF
  • No access to source code needed — tests the system as an attacker would
  • Slower — requires a running environment
  • Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Nuclei

IAST (Interactive Application Security Testing):

  • Instruments the running application to observe behavior from the inside
  • Combines SAST (code awareness) with DAST (runtime testing)
  • Lower false positive rate than SAST
  • Requires agent installation in the application runtime
  • Tools: Contrast Security, Hdiv

SCA (Software Composition Analysis):

  • Scans dependencies for known vulnerabilities (CVEs)
  • Essential — most codebases are 80%+ third-party code
  • Run on every build, monitor continuously
  • Tools: Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check, Grype

Penetration Testing:

  • Skilled security professionals attempt to breach the system using real attacker techniques
  • Tests not just technical vulnerabilities but also process, human, and configuration weaknesses
  • Types: black box (no knowledge of the system), grey box (partial knowledge), white box (full knowledge including source code)
  • Conduct annually or after major architectural changes
  • Findings are prioritized by severity and exploitability

Bug Bounty Programs:

  • Invite external security researchers to find vulnerabilities in exchange for rewards
  • Continuous testing by a global community of diverse skill sets
  • Platforms: HackerOne, Bugcrowd
  • Supplement, don't replace, internal security testing and penetration testing
  • Interview-specific: A mature security testing program uses ALL of these in layers. SAST + SCA in every CI build (shift left). DAST against staging weekly or on every deployment. Penetration testing annually. Bug bounty for continuous external perspective. Each layer catches different vulnerability types.

13. Secrets Management Deep Dive

The Secrets Problem

  • Interview-specific: Secrets (database passwords, API keys, encryption keys, certificates, tokens) are the keys to the kingdom. A single leaked secret can lead to: data breach (database credentials), financial loss (payment API keys), identity impersonation (signing keys), or complete system compromise (admin credentials).

Where Secrets Get Leaked

  • Source code: Hardcoded credentials committed to Git. Even if deleted later, they exist in Git history forever. Public GitHub repositories are continuously scanned by attackers.
  • Configuration files: Config files in plain text on servers or in container images.
  • Environment variables: While better than code, environment variables can leak through process dumps, error messages, logging, and child processes.
  • CI/CD pipeline logs: Secrets echoed in build output or deployment logs.
  • Docker images: Secrets baked into layers during build. Even if deleted in a later layer, they exist in earlier layers.
  • Chat and documentation: Secrets shared via Slack, email, Confluence pages, shared documents.

Secrets Management Solutions

HashiCorp Vault:

  • Interview-specific:
    • Centralized secrets storage, encrypted at rest. Fine-grained access control policies. Complete audit log of all secret access.
    • Dynamic secrets: Vault generates unique, short-lived credentials on demand. A service requests database credentials → Vault creates a temporary database user with a 1-hour TTL → the service uses it → Vault revokes it after 1 hour. If the credential leaks, it's only valid for one service for one hour.
    • Secret engines: Vault can manage different types of secrets — database credentials (generates/revokes dynamically), PKI (issues TLS certificates), AWS credentials (generates temporary IAM credentials), encryption as a service (Transit engine — data never touches disk, encrypted/decrypted in memory).
    • Authentication methods: Vault supports multiple auth methods — Kubernetes service accounts, AWS IAM roles, OIDC, tokens, AppRole. Each service authenticates using its platform identity (K8s pod authenticates with its service account token, EC2 instance authenticates with its IAM role) — no bootstrap secret needed.

Cloud-Native Secrets Managers:

  • AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager.
  • Interview-specific: Simpler than Vault. Fully managed. Tightly integrated with their cloud ecosystem. Automatic rotation for some secret types (RDS passwords in AWS Secrets Manager). Use when you're single-cloud and don't need Vault's advanced features (dynamic secrets, multiple secret engines, cross-cloud support).

Secret Rotation

  • Interview-specific:
    • Secrets should be rotated regularly (every 90 days for most, every 30 days for high-sensitivity). Automated rotation is essential — manual rotation is forgotten, delayed, or done incorrectly.
    • Rotation process: generate new secret → update secret in secrets manager → application picks up new secret (on next connection, via refresh, or via restart) → old secret is retained briefly (dual-active period for in-flight connections) → old secret is revoked.
    • Zero-downtime rotation: the application must handle secret changes without downtime. Either the application watches for secret changes (Vault Agent, SDK notifications), or connections are pooled and new connections use the new credentials while existing connections drain.

14. Privacy Engineering

Privacy by Design

  • Interview-specific: Privacy isn't a compliance checkbox — it's a design principle. The seven foundational principles:
    1. Proactive not Reactive: Anticipate and prevent privacy issues before they occur. Threat model for privacy risks during design, not after a breach.
    2. Privacy as Default: No action required from the user to protect their privacy. Data collection is minimal by default. Sharing is opt-in, not opt-out.
    3. Privacy Embedded in Design: Privacy is built into the architecture, not bolted on. Data minimization, purpose limitation, and access controls are architectural decisions, not afterthoughts.
    4. Full Functionality (Positive-Sum): Privacy doesn't require sacrificing functionality. Find solutions that provide both privacy and functionality (differential privacy for analytics, tokenization for payments).
    5. End-to-End Security: Data is protected throughout its entire lifecycle — collection, processing, storage, transmission, archival, and deletion.
    6. Visibility and Transparency: Users know what data is collected, why, and how it's used. Privacy policies are readable, not legal jargon.
    7. Respect for User Privacy: User interests are the priority. Default settings protect privacy. User-friendly controls for data management.

Data Minimization

  • Interview-specific:
    • Collect only what you need for the stated purpose. Don't collect "just in case."
    • Process only what you need. If you need to verify a user is over 18, you need their birth date — not their full date of birth, name, address, and ID number.
    • Store only what you need. Delete data when the purpose is fulfilled. A completed support ticket doesn't need to retain the customer's payment details.
    • Retain only as long as needed. Define retention periods for every data type. Automate deletion when retention expires.

Anonymization vs Pseudonymization

Anonymization: Irreversible removal of identifying information. Cannot be reversed to identify individuals. Truly anonymized data is no longer personal data under GDPR. Techniques: generalization (age 23 → age range 20-30), suppression (remove identifying fields), noise addition (add random variation), k-anonymity (ensure each record is indistinguishable from k-1 others).

Pseudonymization: Replace identifying information with pseudonyms (tokens, hashes). Can be reversed with the mapping table. Still personal data under GDPR (because it can be re-identified) but reduces risk. The mapping table must be stored separately with strict access controls.

Differential Privacy: Add mathematically calibrated noise to query results. Individual records cannot be inferred from the results, but aggregate statistics remain accurate. Used by: Apple (telemetry), Google (RAPPOR), US Census Bureau. The strongest privacy guarantee for statistical analysis.

  • Interview-specific: "How do you run analytics on user data while protecting privacy?" Answer: pseudonymize data for internal analytics (replace user IDs with tokens, strip direct identifiers). For public or shared datasets, use differential privacy or k-anonymity. For non-production environments, use synthetic data generation (create realistic but entirely fabricated datasets).

Right to Deletion Implementation

  • Interview-specific: The hardest GDPR requirement to implement technically. When a user requests deletion, you must delete their data from:
    • Primary databases (relational, document, graph)
    • Read replicas
    • Caches (Redis, CDN)
    • Search indexes (Elasticsearch)
    • Analytics warehouses and data lakes
    • Message queues and event streams (Kafka — immutable log!)
    • Backups (can you selectively delete from backups?)
    • Log files (if they contain user identifiers)
    • Third-party systems you've shared data with

For event-sourced systems: you can't delete individual events from an immutable log. Solutions: crypto-shredding (encrypt each user's events with a user-specific key; to "delete" the user, delete the key — the events are still there but are unreadable), tombstone events (publish a "UserDataDeleted" event that instructs all projections to purge the user's data).

For Kafka: configure retention to expire events after a reasonable period. For event sourcing with Kafka, use compaction or external event stores that support deletion. This is an area of active architectural challenge.

15. Advanced Security Topics

Security Architecture for Microservices

  • Interview-specific:
    • API Gateway as security perimeter: The gateway handles: TLS termination, authentication (validate JWT/OAuth token), rate limiting, IP blocking, request logging. Backend services receive pre-authenticated requests.
    • Service mesh for internal security: mTLS for all service-to-service communication. Authorization policies per service pair. Traffic encryption without application changes.
    • Defense in depth for microservices: Gateway authentication → Service-level authorization → Database-level access control → Data encryption at rest → Audit logging at every layer.
    • JWT propagation pattern: Gateway validates the JWT and forwards it (or extracted claims) to backend services. Each service makes its own authorization decisions based on claims. Don't have every service re-validate the JWT with the auth server — use the public key for local validation.

Cryptography Essentials (What Architects Need to Know)

  • Interview-specific:
    • Symmetric encryption (AES): Same key for encryption and decryption. Fast. Use for: data at rest, bulk data encryption. AES-256-GCM is the standard. Challenge: how do you share the key securely between parties?
    • Asymmetric encryption (RSA, ECDSA): Public key encrypts, private key decrypts (or private key signs, public key verifies). Slower than symmetric. Use for: key exchange (share symmetric keys securely), digital signatures (JWT signing, code signing), TLS handshake.
    • Hashing (SHA-256, SHA-3): One-way function. Cannot derive the input from the output. Use for: integrity verification (checksums), password storage (with salt and key stretching — bcrypt/argon2), digital signatures.
    • Key stretching (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2): Deliberately slow hashing algorithms for passwords. Make brute-force infeasible. Argon2 is the current recommendation (winner of the Password Hashing Competition, resistant to GPU and ASIC attacks).
    • Don't invent your own cryptography. Use established libraries (libsodium, OpenSSL, cloud KMS). Crypto is one of the few domains where "smart engineers" regularly fail. Use proven implementations.

Zero-Day Vulnerabilities and Response

  • Interview-specific:
    • Zero-day: a vulnerability that is being exploited before a patch is available. You can't patch what doesn't have a patch yet.
    • Defense: defense in depth (multiple layers of security — even if one layer has a zero-day, others protect), network segmentation (contain the blast radius), monitoring and anomaly detection (detect unusual behavior even if you don't know the specific vulnerability), WAF virtual patching (WAF rules can block specific attack patterns while waiting for a software patch), and rapid patching capability (when the patch is released, you can deploy it within hours, not weeks).

Threat Intelligence

  • Interview-specific:
    • Feeds of known malicious indicators: IP addresses, domains, file hashes, URLs, email addresses associated with threat actors.
    • MITRE ATT&CK framework: a comprehensive knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). Organized by attack lifecycle stages (initial access, execution, persistence, privilege escalation, defense evasion, credential access, discovery, lateral movement, collection, exfiltration, impact).
    • Use ATT&CK to: map your detection capabilities against known techniques, identify gaps (which techniques can't you detect?), prioritize detection engineering efforts, and structure red team exercises.

Summary — Interview Signal Map

Topic

Junior Signal

Senior/Architect Signal

Mindset

"Add authentication"

Threat modeling with STRIDE, defense-in-depth layering, attack surface management

Authentication

"Use JWT"

OAuth2 grant types, OIDC vs OAuth, JWT validation steps, token storage security, passwordless

Authorization

"Check roles"

RBAC vs ABAC vs ReBAC trade-offs, OPA for externalized policies, resource-level authorization

Network

"Use HTTPS"

Zero trust, mTLS, micro-segmentation, WAF, defense-in-depth layers

Application

"Sanitize inputs"

OWASP Top 10 with specific mitigations, SSRF/BOLA awareness, security headers

Data

"Encrypt the database"

Envelope encryption, key hierarchy, tokenization, data classification, field-level encryption

API

"Rate limit"

BOLA prevention, GraphQL security, input/output validation, per-endpoint authorization

Infrastructure

"Scan containers"

Pod security, admission controllers, supply chain (SBOM, signing, SLSA), IaC scanning

CI/CD

"Run tests"

SAST + SCA + DAST in pipeline, pipeline hardening, immutable artifacts, separation of duties

Incidents

"Fix the bug"

IR framework (preparation through post-mortem), SIEM, detection engineering, breach notification

Compliance

"Follow the rules"

GDPR technical implementation, PCI scope reduction, SOC 2 preparation, compliance as code

Secrets

"Use env vars"

Vault dynamic secrets, rotation automation, zero-downtime rotation, crypto-shredding

Privacy

"Anonymize data"

Privacy by design, differential privacy, right to deletion architecture, data minimization

Testing

"Run a pen test"

Layered testing (SAST/DAST/SCA/pen test/bug bounty), shift left, continuous security validation
